# Questions in Livestream 236

Live Stream #236: Our Third ETS D&D Special Live Stream

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=5241" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:21</a> | Was the economy really that bad at ETS this year? | Yes. He says the budget strain was obvious, with no free books or normal publisher promotion events. | He says the downturn was obvious in what was offered at ETS: there were no free books that year, so he had nothing to show as publisher freebies. He describes the situation as "not good" and connects it to a visibly reduced conference economy, though he still bought several language books himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=5417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:17</a> | Is ancient Ethiopic another name for Ge'ez? | Yes. He says ancient Ethiopic is another name for Ge'ez. | He answers directly that ancient Ethiopic is Ge'ez, then moves back to discussing the language books he bought at the Zondervan booth. The transcript does not add a longer technical explanation beyond confirming the equivalence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=5763" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:03</a> | If the 80 years between Moses's birth and the Exodus are taken literally, would that solve the chronology issue? | He says no; even a literalist framing still leaves an extra 40 years in the proposed chronology. | He says even a literalist or fundamentalist reading would not normally treat the disputed span as a full 80-year solution, because Moses's relevant age would be treated as 40 in that context. That leaves an extra 40 years still unaccounted for, so the proposal does not solve the chronological problem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=5798" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:38</a> | Why would making Ahmose the oppression pharaoh mean Aaron and Amram also had to die? | He says Aaron would also fall under a long-running decree against baby boys, while Amram is less certain. | He says Amram is less clear, but Aaron is the problem because Aaron was only three years older than Moses. If Ahmose began a killing campaign against baby boys that continued for the whole earlier period, Aaron would have fallen under that death sentence too; Aaron only avoids the decree if it began around Moses's birth and applied to boys two and under. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=5893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:13</a> | Isn't IP going to have a talk on the evidence for the Exodus? | He is not sure. He says IP did ETS and then went on to the Sound Faith conference. | He says he does not know whether IP is giving that specific talk. He had met and talked with IP at ETS, and says IP did ETS and then went on to Sound Faith, where IP had invited him, but he could not attend because he was at ASOR. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=5987" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:47</a> | Any drama at ASOR? | Yes. He says there was drama there too, including a verbal altercation at ASOR over Ron Wyatt-style Exodus claims. | He says there was ASOR drama and begins with a verbal altercation involving a man connected to Mary Nell Wyatt, Ron Wyatt's widow. The issue was a claim that a site or figure was Moses; he objected and says the conversation escalated around Ron Wyatt-style Exodus claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=7272" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:12</a> | Would Christian nationalism automatically lead to totalitarianism? | He says yes, distinguishing it from patriotism and arguing that it tends toward coercive cultural control. | He distinguishes Christian patriotism from Christian nationalism, saying one can love the flag, constitution, government, and balance of powers consistently as a Christian. Christian nationalism, in his framing, becomes totalitarian because it tries to turn a multicultural society into a cultural monolith, specifically a white Protestant theocracy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=7370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:50</a> | Would most states even follow Stephen Wolfe's Christian nationalist program? | He says he can imagine many states going along with it because the movement has gained traction, and he frames it as authoritarian. | He says he can imagine many states going along because the movement has already gained significant traction. He groups Wolfe with broader authoritarian nationalist figures and argues that the appeal is the ability to impose a desired nation, which he links with antisemitism and authoritarianism in that political stream. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=8103" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:03</a> | Where can I find your Egyptian chronology and translations of the Anastasi, Sallier, Leiden, and Harris papyri? | He points to `roundhogchronology.com` for his chronology, recommends Caminos for Anastasi and Sallier, Zandee for Leiden, and says Papyrus Harris has several usable translations. | He gives `roundhogchronology.com` for his Egyptian chronology. For Anastasi and Sallier he recommends Caminos's late Egyptian miscellanies, for Leiden he recommends Zandee while warning the volume is scarce, and for Papyrus Harris he says Harris I is well known enough that several decent translations exist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=8280" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:00</a> | What books or resources on Egypt would you recommend for a better understanding of the Exodus? | He recommends Kitchen's *On the Reliability of the Old Testament* and says Hoffmeier's revised *Egypt and the Bible* should also be useful when it appears. | He still recommends Kitchen's *On the Reliability of the Old Testament*. He also says Hoffmeier told him the revised second edition of *Egypt and the Bible* was almost done, would move from Oxford to Hendrickson, and would add roughly 40 percent more material; he also mentions hoping to produce his own book on the historical and cultural context of the Exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=8348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:08</a> | Do we know the average age of men marrying in the 18th century BC? What about women? | He says women may have averaged around 18, while men often married later, perhaps around 25, because they needed to be financially established. | He says the answer depends, but women in the ancient world likely averaged around 18, with some marrying at 16, 17, 19, 20, or 21. Men usually married later because they needed financial independence and a trade or resources sufficient to support a family, so late 20s or early 30s could happen, with about 25 as his rough average for the 18th century BC. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=8485" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:25</a> | Did Egyptians have a soul-framework similar to the Israelite idea of nephesh being the throat? | No. He says Egyptians used overlapping soul-components such as the ka, ba, and akh rather than that kind of anatomical mapping. | He says Egyptians did not map the soul to body parts the way Israelites could speak of nephesh, kidneys, or heart. Instead, Egyptian anthropology used overlapping components such as the ka as life force, the ba as emotions or seat of emotions, and the akh as intellect, with other components also possible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=8625" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:45</a> | When Abraham dies, does Isaac take over Abraham's clan, property, and wealth, or does he create his own? | He says Isaac was supposed to inherit the main clan and most of the wealth, though Abraham also became progenitor to multiple clan lines. | He says Isaac is supposed to take over Abraham's clan, property, and wealth, and in all likelihood receives most of the slaves, animals, and inheritance because he has the blessing and birthright. But he qualifies that Abraham becomes progenitor of multiple clan lines, so later memory can also treat Isaac as a new progenitor distinct from Ishmael and the sons of Keturah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=8846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:26</a> | Did ancient Near Eastern Israelites regard the human voice as sacred because it originates from the physical nephesh? | He says no. The voice could be used for sacred acts, but it was not inherently treated as sacred. | He says the human voice could be used in sacred acts such as chanting, praising the Lord, reciting scripture harmoniously, or singing psalms. But he does not see evidence that the voice itself was regarded as inherently sacred, even if it originates from the physical nephesh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=8905" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:25</a> | How should Hebrews 11:27 be reconciled with Exodus 2, where Moses is said to be afraid? | He says Exodus 2 only states that Moses was afraid because the killing had become known, not explicitly that he feared Pharaoh, so he does not see a contradiction. | He reads Hebrews 11:27 and Exodus 2:14 together and argues that Exodus says Moses was afraid because the matter had become known, not specifically that he feared Pharaoh. He suggests Moses may have feared prosecution, jail, or surviving witnesses, especially under a law-and-order pharaoh, while Hebrews says he did not ultimately act from fear of Pharaoh's wrath. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=9366" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:06</a> | How long after Sarah's death do you think Abraham remarried? | He says the text does not give enough information to know; it could have been years or decades. | He checks Sarah's age at death as 127 from Genesis 23:1, but says Keturah's age and the timing of her children are not supplied. Because the text does not give the needed data, he treats any estimate as speculation and says the gap could have been years or decades. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=10107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:27</a> | What is the relevance of Samson's strength and his hair? | He says Samson's strength came from the Spirit of the Lord in connection with his Nazirite vow, not from magical power in the hair itself. | He argues Samson's strength is not located in the hair as magic, but in the Spirit of the Lord being upon him while he remains under the Nazirite vow. Samson has already broken the wine and corpse-related provisions; cutting the hair breaks the last provision, so the Spirit departs and Samson becomes like everyone else. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=10430" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:50</a> | Did God intend Samson to be a polemic against Heracles or similar heroes? | He says probably not; apart from both being strong figures, the stories and settings are too different. | He says he does not think Samson is intended as a polemic against Heracles. The main overlap is that both are strong men, but their biographies are very different, he does not see a cultural connection, and if the Samson story goes back to the time of the judges it would also be hundreds of years earlier than the Heracles material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=10530" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:30</a> | Can arguing about Greek and Hebrew get in the way of seeing what the biblical author is trying to say? | He says the bigger problem is usually bad use or loss of the languages, not the languages themselves; knowing them generally helps interpretation. | He says he more often sees the opposite problem: people argue from English wording and miss the underlying Greek or Hebrew. He allows that poor training, forgotten nuance, or one-year language knowledge can lead to bad arguments, especially with things like genitives and participles, but says good knowledge of the biblical languages is a necessary tool for exegesis and expository preaching. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=11176" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:16</a> | Was the swastika originally a church symbol? | He says some Christian groups used the bent cross before World War II, though in a different form from the Nazi emblem, and other religions used it too. | He says some denominations and Christian objects used the bent-cross form, including old Anglican prayer books, but not necessarily in the same orientation or angle as the Nazi emblem. He also notes Buddhist use and says the symbol carried different meanings before World War II, after which National Socialist appropriation loaded it with its modern negative connotations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=11742" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:42</a> | Is the wider context of a Bible book more important than the Greek or Hebrew word used in a verse? | He says neither outranks the other; grammar, local context, genre, whole-book context, and salvation-history context all matter together. | He says biblical interpretation has many contexts: historical, grammatical, genre, chapter or local context, thematic context of the whole book, and salvation-history context. The Greek or Hebrew wording is part of grammatical context and matters, but so does the rest of the book; he warns against isolating John 3:16 from the rest of John 3 because that can become proof texting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=12061" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:01</a> | How did the Greeks view the soul and anatomy? | He sketches a broadly Greek philosophical view in which the soul was created, endured beyond death, and was discussed in relation to body and afterlife rather than Israelite-style body-part language. | He says he cannot really answer the anatomy part, then sketches Aristotle, Socrates, and a Pythagorean-style view. In that account the soul is created, not eternal backward in time, but normally endures forward after death; he describes souls as sparks associated with the gods, human bodies, possible loss after death, and Greek afterlife ideas such as Hades or the Elysian fields. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=12255" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:15</a> | Why are Satan and Michael disputing over Moses's body in Jude 9? | He says Jude's point is not to explain the reason for the dispute, but to model rebuke without reviling. | He says Jude draws on a Jewish apocryphal example after a passage about rebellious people who defile flesh, reject authority, and revile angelic majesties. Jude does not explain why Satan and Michael dispute over Moses's body; the point is that Michael does not revile or attack but says, "The Lord rebuke you," modeling rebuke without slander or violence when evil cannot simply be stopped. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=12695" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:35</a> | In Genesis 35:27-29, what happens to Isaac's clan when he dies? | He says Jacob had the blessing and leadership, while Esau formed his own line, so Isaac's clan either merged into Jacob's, fractured, or re-formed under another progenitor. | He distinguishes the birthright as the inheritance or "stuff" from the blessing as public recognition of clan leadership. Jacob bought the birthright and stole the blessing, so Esau has neither the goods nor recognized leadership; Esau leaves and forms his own tribe, while Jacob returns, and Isaac's old clan either integrates into Jacob's, dissolves, or continues under another progenitor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=13118" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:38</a> | Is it fair to label Jacob a trickster or deceiver in this narrative? | Yes. He says Jacob earns that reputation through deception and concealment, even if the name itself can also carry other senses. | He says the label is fair, especially "deceiver," because Jacob deceives Isaac with Rebekah's help, withholds from Laban what he is doing with the flocks, and leaves Laban under cover of opportunity. He also notes that Jacob's name can have more than one sense, including a theophoric "God helps" form, but the narrative gives him a reputation that fits deception. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/r7A_KLa5PBs?t=13379" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:59</a> | What is a blessing? | He says a blessing is an appeal for God's favor, especially favor tied to leadership and inheritance. | He defines a blessing, in the Jacob and firstborn context, as an appeal or oath to God asking that a chosen child receive God's special favor. In an ancient clan setting it can be given to only one child and functions as a sign of leadership, comparable in some ways to an anointing or coronation blessing for a ruler. |
